past_medical_history: Currently pregnant at 15 weeks and 5 days of gestation (primiparous)
No history of hypertension, diabetes, or cardiac disease

medications.drug: Prenatal multivitamin with iron and folic acid

family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 55
Mother alive and well with no significant medical history
No family history of stroke, venous thromboembolism, or bleeding disorders

social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Mild daily walking, otherwise sedentary during pregnancy

neurological_exam.mental_status: Obtunded. Glasgow Coma Scale 10 (E3 V2 M5). Disoriented to time, place, and situation. Produces incomprehensible sounds only; no recognizable words or phrases. Does not follow verbal commands reliably. Unable to assess higher cortical functions due to reduced level of consciousness.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally; right pupil briskly reactive, left pupil sluggishly reactive to light. No ptosis. No apparent facial asymmetry at rest. Corneal reflexes intact bilaterally. Gag reflex intact. Tongue midline on protrusion. No nystagmus observed at rest.
neurological_exam.motor: Increased tone in all four extremities, more prominent on the left. Left upper extremity: 2/5 strength with withdrawal to pain only. Left lower extremity: 3/5 strength at hip flexion, 2/5 at ankle dorsiflexion. Right upper extremity: 4/5 strength. Right lower extremity: 4/5 strength. Bilateral upgoing plantar responses (positive Babinski sign bilaterally), more pronounced on the left.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased response to pinprick stimulation on the left hemibody compared to right. Proprioception and vibration sense not reliably testable due to altered consciousness.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 3+, right lower extremity 3+. Left upper extremity 3+, left lower extremity 4+ with sustained ankle clonus on the left. Bilateral extensor plantar responses.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess finger-to-nose or heel-to-shin testing due to reduced consciousness and left-sided weakness.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate due to reduced level of consciousness and left-sided weakness.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score estimated at 22. No meningismus. No papilledema on bedside fundoscopy. Gravid uterus consistent with second-trimester pregnancy.

patient.chief_complaint: Acute onset headache, vomiting, reduced responsiveness, and left-sided weakness
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Priya Nair is a 33-year-old right-handed woman, currently at 15 weeks and 5 days of gestation in her second pregnancy (primiparous, one prior cesarean delivery), who was brought to the emergency department by her husband after acute onset of bilateral frontotemporal headache associated with dizziness and blurred vision approximately 18 hours prior to presentation. She initially attributed her symptoms to pregnancy-related fatigue and rested at home. Over the next several hours, she developed two episodes of non-projectile, non-bloody vomiting. Her husband reports that approximately 6 hours ago, she became progressively less responsive, began producing incomprehensible speech instead of her usual fluent communication, and then suddenly developed weakness in her left arm that rapidly progressed to involve her left leg. He immediately brought her to the emergency department. On arrival, the patient appeared drowsy and distressed, making unintelligible vocalizations but unable to communicate meaningfully. She was moving her right extremities spontaneously but had minimal movement of the left arm and reduced movement of the left leg. There was no history of head trauma, seizure activity, urinary incontinence, chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, or vaginal bleeding. She has no prior history of neurological symptoms, migraines, or thromboembolic events. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated to date, with normal first-trimester screening and ultrasound. She has no known vascular risk factors aside from the hypercoagulable state of pregnancy. Her last known neurologically normal time is approximately 6 hours prior to emergency department arrival, when she was still speaking coherently before the acute decline in responsiveness and onset of focal weakness.

findings.location: Right pons and right middle cerebellar peduncle
findings.size: Approximately 2.8 x 1.5 cm in the right pons, extending into the right middle cerebellar peduncle

signal_characteristics.DWI: Markedly hyperintense signal indicating restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in the right pons and right middle cerebellar peduncle, consistent with subacute ischemia (onset >6 hours)
signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle hypointensity in the affected regions
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the right pons and right middle cerebellar peduncle

findings.mass_effect: No significant mass effect. No obstructive hydrocephalus. Fourth ventricle is normal in size and configuration.
findings.borders: Well-defined margins conforming to posterior circulation vascular territory

findings.location: Basilar artery with concurrent right vertebral artery V3 segment obstruction

signal_characteristics.MRA: Complete occlusion of the basilar artery with absent flow signal. Obstruction of the V3 segment of the right vertebral artery with diminished distal flow.
signal_characteristics.TOF: Loss of flow-related enhancement in the basilar artery. Reduced flow signal in the right vertebral artery V3 segment.

additional_observations: Left vertebral artery is patent with normal caliber and flow
Anterior circulation (bilateral ICAs, MCAs, ACAs) patent without stenosis on MRA
No evidence of venous sinus thrombosis on available sequences
Normal appearance of the pituitary gland
mri.impression: 1. Acute ischemic infarction in the right pons and right middle cerebellar peduncle with restricted diffusion. 2. Occlusion of the basilar artery on MRA. 3. Obstruction of the V3 segment of the right vertebral artery. 4. No hemorrhagic transformation. 5. Patent anterior circulation. Clinical correlation recommended; findings are consistent with posterior circulation ischemic stroke secondary to basilar artery occlusion.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction with ADC confirmation in posterior circulation territory, basilar artery occlusion on MRA
Lesion confined to vascular territory, no periventricular or juxtacortical lesions, no enhancement pattern typical of MS
Vascular territory distribution and associated vessel occlusion favor ischemic etiology over inflammatory process

recommended_actions: Urgent neurology and neurointerventional consultation for consideration of endovascular therapy for basilar artery occlusion
CT angiography of head and neck for further vascular characterization
Obstetric consultation for fetal monitoring and pregnancy-safe treatment planning
Hypercoagulability workup given young age and pregnancy

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 104 bpm. Otherwise normal ECG. No arrhythmia or acute ischemic changes detected.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Sinus tachycardia is likely physiological in the setting of pregnancy and acute neurological stress. No primary cardiac arrhythmia identified as embolic source. Further cardiac evaluation may be warranted to assess for structural heart disease or patent foramen ovale as potential embolic pathways.

CBC.clinical_significance: Leukocytosis, may reflect acute stress response or physiological leukocytosis of pregnancy
Neutrophilia, consistent with acute physiological stress
Mild anemia, likely physiological hemodilution of pregnancy

BMP.clinical_significance: Hyponatremia, may be dilutional in pregnancy or related to vomiting and poor oral intake
Mild hypokalemia, likely related to vomiting episodes
Low, consistent with losses from vomiting
Low-normal, consistent with increased renal clearance in pregnancy
Mildly elevated, likely stress hyperglycemia in the acute setting

Coagulation.clinical_significance: Elevated fibrinogen, consistent with pregnancy-associated hypercoagulable state; may contribute to thrombotic risk

Infectious and Autoimmune.clinical_significance: Elevated, but physiologically expected in pregnancy
Mildly elevated, nonspecific; may reflect acute stress or pregnancy

labs.interpretation: Laboratory findings show leukocytosis with neutrophilia likely reflecting acute stress and pregnancy physiology, mild anemia consistent with pregnancy-related hemodilution, hyponatremia and hypokalemia attributable to vomiting and poor oral intake, elevated fibrinogen consistent with pregnancy-associated hypercoagulable state, and mildly elevated inflammatory markers. Coagulation studies are otherwise normal. Dengue serologies are negative, effectively ruling out dengue-associated complications. The elevated fibrinogen in the context of pregnancy supports evaluation for pregnancy-related thrombotic risk as a contributing factor to ischemic stroke.

signal_characteristics.CTA: Complete occlusion of the mid-to-distal basilar artery with absent opacification. Thrombus length estimated at approximately 12 mm.
Occlusion of the right vertebral artery at the V3 segment. No flow reconstitution distally. Left vertebral artery is patent and dominant.

additional_observations: Left vertebral artery is dominant and patent throughout its course
Bilateral internal carotid arteries are patent without stenosis
Bilateral MCAs and ACAs are patent
No atherosclerotic stenosis in the anterior circulation
output.impression: 1. Complete occlusion of the mid-to-distal basilar artery confirmed on CTA, consistent with acute basilar artery thrombosis. 2. Right vertebral artery V3 segment occlusion. 3. Patent anterior circulation and left vertebral artery. Emergent neurointerventional consultation recommended for consideration of endovascular thrombectomy.
recommended_actions: Emergent neurointerventional consultation for basilar artery thrombectomy
Multidisciplinary discussion with obstetrics regarding procedural risks during pregnancy
Consider IV thrombolysis as bridge to thrombectomy with obstetric risk assessment

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: Mildly hypermobile interatrial septum; bubble study suggested for PFO evaluation
Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mildly hypermobile septum may warrant bubble study or TEE to assess for patent foramen ovale as potential paradoxical embolism route

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates normal biventricular size and systolic function. No valvular abnormalities. The interatrial septum appears mildly hypermobile, raising the possibility of a patent foramen ovale. A bubble study or transesophageal echocardiogram is recommended for further evaluation. No intracardiac thrombus visualized. No structural cardiac abnormality identified as a definitive embolic source.
abnormal_values_summary: Mildly hypermobile interatrial septum — consider bubble study or TEE for PFO evaluation

findings: Normal sinus rhythm throughout the 48-hour recording
No episodes of atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other supraventricular tachycardia
No significant pauses (longest pause 1.4 seconds during sleep)
Rare premature atrial complexes (18 in 48 hours)
output.interpretation: Forty-eight hour Holter monitoring reveals normal sinus rhythm throughout the recording with no arrhythmias detected. No evidence of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation or other cardiac arrhythmia as an embolic source.
output.clinical_correlation: Absence of arrhythmia on extended monitoring makes cardiac arrhythmia an unlikely embolic source. The stroke etiology may be related to pregnancy-associated hypercoagulability, vertebral artery pathology, or paradoxical embolism if PFO is confirmed. Further evaluation recommended.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Borderline low, may be artifactually decreased in acute thrombosis and pregnancy; repeat testing recommended in 12 weeks
Low, but protein S is physiologically decreased during pregnancy and may be consumed during acute thrombosis; repeat post-partum

output.interpretation: Antiphospholipid antibodies are negative. No hereditary thrombophilia mutations detected (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation). Protein C is borderline low and Protein S is reduced, but both are expected to decrease during pregnancy and acute thrombotic events. These results should be repeated at least 12 weeks postpartum to assess for a true deficiency. The overall assessment is that pregnancy-associated hypercoagulability is the most likely contributing thrombotic risk factor in this patient, with elevated fibrinogen supporting a prothrombotic state.
abnormal_values_summary: Protein C Activity 68% (borderline low — may be artifactual in pregnancy/acute thrombosis)
Protein S Activity 42% (low — physiologically decreased in pregnancy)

findings.location: Bilateral common carotid arteries and internal carotid arteries

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries demonstrate normal flow velocities and waveform morphology
No intimal thickening or plaque identified bilaterally
Bilateral vertebral arteries: left vertebral artery patent with antegrade flow; right vertebral artery with markedly diminished flow, corroborating known V3 segment occlusion
Normal external carotid artery flow bilaterally
output.impression: 1. Normal bilateral carotid arteries without stenosis or atherosclerotic plaque. 2. Right vertebral artery with diminished flow consistent with known V3 segment occlusion. 3. Left vertebral artery patent with normal antegrade flow. Findings are consistent with posterior circulation pathology without anterior circulation involvement.
recommended_actions: Focus of vascular evaluation should remain on posterior circulation and basilar artery

output.query: acute ischemic stroke pregnancy management thrombolysis thrombectomy

results.title: Acute Ischemic Stroke in Pregnancy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
results.authors: Swartz RH, Cayley ML, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase has been used in pregnancy with acceptable safety profile in case series, though it does not cross the placenta. Decision should involve multidisciplinary team.

results.title: Mechanical Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke During Pregnancy: A Systematic Review
results.journal: Journal of Stroke and Cerebrovascular Diseases
results.key_finding: Mechanical thrombectomy is feasible during pregnancy with favorable maternal outcomes. Radiation exposure should be minimized with abdominal shielding.

results.title: AHA/ASA Scientific Statement: Management of Stroke in Pregnancy and the Puerperium
results.authors: Too G, Wen T, et al.
results.key_finding: Pregnancy should not be an absolute contraindication to IV thrombolysis or mechanical thrombectomy. Benefits of treatment generally outweigh risks in large vessel occlusion.

output.summary: Current evidence supports that IV alteplase and mechanical thrombectomy can be safely used in pregnant patients with acute ischemic stroke. Alteplase does not cross the placenta and has been used in pregnancy-related stroke in multiple case series with acceptable outcomes. Mechanical thrombectomy is the preferred approach for large vessel occlusion, including basilar artery occlusion, with radiation exposure minimized using abdominal lead shielding. Multidisciplinary management involving neurology, neurointerventional radiology, and obstetrics is essential. Anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin is preferred over warfarin or DOACs for secondary prevention during pregnancy.

interactions: Alteplase is FDA pregnancy category C — animal studies show adverse effects but no adequate human studies
Alteplase does not cross the placenta due to its large molecular weight
Concurrent use with antiplatelet agents increases bleeding risk
contraindications: Recent (within 3 months) intracranial or spinal surgery or serious head trauma
warnings: Pregnancy is listed as a relative contraindication in some guidelines, but AHA/ASA states benefits may outweigh risks in LVO
Risk of placental abruption and uterine bleeding exists but is rare in reported cases
Close obstetric monitoring required during and after administration
Fibrinogen levels should be monitored as a marker of consumptive coagulopathy
output.formulary_status: Available — requires multidisciplinary approval for use in pregnancy
alternatives: Mechanical thrombectomy alone (avoids systemic thrombolysis risk)
Tenecteplase (single-bolus, same pregnancy considerations as alteplase)
Low-molecular-weight heparin (for secondary prevention, not acute reperfusion)

output.query: basilar artery occlusion endovascular thrombectomy outcomes

results.title: Endovascular Therapy for Acute Basilar-Artery Occlusion (ATTENTION Trial)
results.authors: Tao C, Li R, et al.
results.key_finding: Endovascular thrombectomy plus medical management was superior to medical management alone for basilar artery occlusion within 12 hours, with improved functional outcomes.

results.title: Trial of Endovascular Therapy for Acute Basilar-Artery Occlusion (BAOCHE)
results.key_finding: Thrombectomy for basilar artery occlusion within 24 hours improved functional outcomes. Modified Rankin Scale 0-3 at 90 days: 46% thrombectomy vs 24% control.

results.title: Basilar Artery Occlusion: Clinical Management and Prognosis
results.authors: Mattle HP, Arnold M, et al.
results.key_finding: Untreated basilar artery occlusion carries mortality >80%. Early recanalization is the strongest predictor of favorable outcome.

output.summary: Recent landmark trials (ATTENTION 2023, BAOCHE 2023) have established endovascular thrombectomy as the standard of care for acute basilar artery occlusion, demonstrating superior functional outcomes compared to medical management alone. The treatment window extends up to 24 hours from symptom onset. Untreated basilar artery occlusion has very high mortality (>80%), making timely intervention critical. For this patient, thrombectomy should be strongly considered given the confirmed basilar artery occlusion and presentation within a treatable time window.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, posterior circulation, due to basilar artery occlusion in the setting of pregnancy-associated hypercoagulability

differential.key_distinguishing: Initial presentation with headache, vomiting, and altered consciousness could mimic meningoencephalitis; however, focal lateralizing deficits, vascular territory infarction on MRI, and basilar artery occlusion on MRA confirm ischemic stroke. No fever, no meningismus, and dengue serologies are negative.
Non-contrast CT showed no acute intracranial hemorrhage. MRI demonstrates ischemic pattern (DWI restriction) rather than hemorrhage. No T1 hyperintensity to suggest blood products.

Pregnancy is a risk factor for CVST and can present with headache and focal deficits; however, MRI findings are in an arterial vascular territory, MRA shows arterial occlusion, and no venous sinus thrombosis was identified on available sequences.

Patient is only 15 weeks pregnant (preeclampsia typically presents after 20 weeks), blood pressure is 130/70 (not hypertensive), no proteinuria, and MRI shows vascular territory infarction rather than the bilateral posterior white matter edema typical of PRES.

No prior migraine history. Symptoms are persistent and progressive rather than episodic and self-limited. MRI confirms structural infarction with vessel occlusion, excluding a functional migrainous etiology.

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code and perform rapid neurological assessment including GCS, NIHSS, and fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: GCS 10, NIHSS ~22, glucose within acceptable range

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to rule out hemorrhage
Obtain stat laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, and fibrinogen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis, hyponatremia, hypokalemia, elevated fibrinogen, normal coagulation times

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia as embolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sinus tachycardia, no atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmia

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with DWI and MRA to characterize the ischemic lesion and vascular occlusion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute infarction in right pons and right cerebellar peduncle with basilar artery occlusion and right vertebral artery V3 occlusion

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck to further characterize basilar artery occlusion and plan endovascular intervention
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complete basilar artery occlusion confirmed, thrombus length approximately 12 mm

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurointerventional radiology for emergent mechanical thrombectomy of basilar artery occlusion
Consult obstetrics for fetal monitoring, pregnancy-safe treatment planning, and abdominal lead shielding during endovascular procedure
Consider IV alteplase as bridge to thrombectomy after multidisciplinary risk-benefit discussion (pregnancy is relative contraindication, not absolute)
Correct electrolyte abnormalities (hyponatremia, hypokalemia) with careful IV fluid and electrolyte replacement
Obtain hypercoagulability workup including antiphospholipid antibodies, protein C, protein S, and genetic thrombophilia testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Antiphospholipid antibodies negative, protein C borderline low, protein S low (expected in pregnancy)

optimal_actions.action: Initiate therapeutic anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin for secondary stroke prevention (preferred over warfarin/DOACs in pregnancy)
Admit to neurological ICU for close monitoring with serial neurological assessments and fetal monitoring

critical_actions: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage
Identify basilar artery occlusion on MRI/MRA or CTA as the stroke mechanism
Consult neurointerventional radiology for emergent mechanical thrombectomy
Involve obstetrics for multidisciplinary pregnancy-safe management
Initiate appropriate anticoagulation (LMWH) for secondary prevention in pregnancy
Admit to ICU with serial neurological and fetal monitoring
contraindicated_actions: Administering warfarin during pregnancy (teratogenic, crosses placenta)
Administering direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) during pregnancy (insufficient safety data, potential teratogenicity)
Aggressively lowering blood pressure in acute posterior circulation stroke (risk of worsening brainstem ischemia)
Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of posterior fossa infarction with risk of herniation
Delaying thrombectomy consultation to obtain additional non-urgent workup in basilar artery occlusion
Administering contrast-enhanced CT unnecessarily without abdominal shielding in pregnancy
key_reasoning_points: Acute onset headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, and lateralizing motor deficits in a young pregnant woman should raise immediate concern for cerebrovascular event, even without traditional vascular risk factors
Pregnancy is an independent risk factor for arterial ischemic stroke due to the physiological hypercoagulable state, particularly elevated fibrinogen and reduced protein S

Viral meningoencephalitis is a reasonable initial differential given headache, vomiting, and altered consciousness, but focal lateralizing deficits (left-sided weakness, bilateral Babinski signs) and vascular territory infarction on MRI argue strongly for ischemic stroke

MRI demonstrates acute pontine and cerebellar infarction with DWI restriction, and MRA reveals basilar artery occlusion — this is a posterior circulation large vessel occlusion requiring emergent intervention
Basilar artery occlusion carries very high mortality (>80%) without intervention; recent trials (ATTENTION, BAOCHE 2023) support thrombectomy up to 24 hours from onset
Pregnancy is a relative, not absolute, contraindication to IV thrombolysis and thrombectomy; multidisciplinary management is essential

Antiphospholipid syndrome must be excluded as a reversible cause of pregnancy-associated stroke; negative serologies in this case support pregnancy-related hypercoagulability as the primary risk factor
Secondary prevention in pregnancy requires low-molecular-weight heparin rather than warfarin (teratogenic) or DOACs (insufficient safety data)

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward — young pregnant woman with acute onset focal neurological deficits, MRI clearly shows posterior circulation infarction with basilar artery occlusion on MRA. Despite the atypical demographics (young, no traditional risk factors), the imaging findings are unambiguous and the diagnosis is readily apparent once diagnostic tools are utilized.
